blocks.body: Sentence Transformations — Tenses shows how to change tense during a rewrite without losing the meaning, the time signal, or the logic of events.

{"level":"B1-B2","intro":"In tense-based transformation tasks, the key is to keep the time perspective: what happened earlier, what still continues now, and what belongs to a finished moment in the past.","rules":[{"label":"TIME REFERENCE","color":"emerald","text":"Start by finding the finished time marker, the duration, or the earlier past event. That usually tells you whether you need Simple, Perfect, or Perfect Continuous.","example":"yesterday / for three years / by the time"},{"label":"MEANING BEFORE FORM","color":"blue","text":"Do not swap a form mechanically. Check whether the sentence expresses a present result, an ongoing duration, or a sequence in the past.","example":"I have known her for years."},{"label":"WATCH THE SMALL WORDS","color":"amber","text":"Tense rewrites are often controlled by since, for, already, yet, just, before, after, by the time, the last time.","example":"I haven't seen him since Monday."}]}

blocks.heading: 1. How to Spot the Needed Rewrite
blocks.body:
Start with the time signal, not with the verb form.

  • If the action started in the past and still continues now, you often need Present Perfect or Present Perfect Continuous.
  • If one action happened before another past action, transformation tasks often require Past Perfect.
  • If the sentence contains a finished time marker such as yesterday, last year, or in 2022, Present Perfect is usually the wrong choice.
  • Pairs such as the last time, it's the first time, and started ... ago often trigger standard exam rewrites.

blocks.heading: 2. Core Transformation Patterns
blocks.body:
Base sentenceTarget sentenceRule focus
I started learning French three years ago.I have been learning French for three years.ago -> for; the action still continues now.
The last time I saw Marta was in May.I haven't seen Marta since May.the last time ... was -> haven't ... since.
When we arrived, the film started.By the time we arrived, the film had started.Past Perfect for the earlier past action.
It is the first time he has driven on the left.He has never driven on the left before.the first time -> never ... before.

In exam tasks, check whether only the tense changes or whether the marker also changes: ago, for, since, before, already, yet.

{"title":"3. Common mistakes","items":[{"label":"Mistake 1","color":"rose","title":"Using Present Perfect with a finished past marker.","wrong":"I have seen him yesterday.","right":"I saw him yesterday.","hint":"After yesterday, last week, in 2021, you usually need Past Simple."},{"label":"Mistake 2","color":"amber","title":"Confusing for and since.","wrong":"She has lived here since five years.","right":"She has lived here for five years.","hint":"For = duration, since = starting point."},{"label":"Mistake 3","color":"sky","title":"Using Past Perfect with no second past reference point.","wrong":"Yesterday I had gone to the shop.","right":"Yesterday I went to the shop.","hint":"Past Perfect usually needs a later past event or a clear past frame."},{"label":"Mistake 4","color":"rose","title":"Losing the idea of duration when the action still continues.","wrong":"I learn English for three years.","right":"I have been learning English for three years.","hint":"If the action began earlier and still continues now, a perfect form is usually needed."}]}

{"title":"4. Quick Exam Check","items":["Mark the time signal before you change the verb form.","Check whether the action still continues now or belongs to a finished past point.","If there are two past actions, decide which one happened earlier.","After rewriting, read the whole sentence again: does the meaning still match, and do since / for / before / by the time sound natural?"]}
